findings.description: Textbook N+1 in monthly_revenue_report: one query fetches paid orders (line 104), then a separate SELECT on order_items fires per order inside the loop, so a month with N paid orders costs N+1 round-trips when a single JOIN/GROUP BY (or pure SQL SUM) would do it in one query with zero Python row iteration.

Non-sargable date filter: strftime('%Y', paid_at)=? and strftime('%m', paid_at)=? wrap the column in a function, defeating any index on paid_at and forcing a full table scan; a half-open range predicate (paid_at >= start AND paid_at < next_month) is index-eligible and avoids per-row string formatting.

charge_order issues SELECT * on orders but only consumes discount_pct (id is already known), over-fetching every column (including the wide tx_id/status row) on each charge; select just the needed column.

A fresh sqlite3.connect() is opened per public call with no pooling/reuse; cheap for SQLite's in-process driver so low impact, but under high call volume the repeated connect/close and pragma re-init add avoidable latency.

_load_items materializes all rows into a list via fetchall() then a list comprehension; fine for normal orders but for pathologically large carts a row-by-row iterator would cap peak memory (micro/style — only matters at extreme item counts).
Out of perf scope (defer to critic-security): logger.info writes the full card_number and cvv in plaintext to the payments log on every charge — PCI-DSS violation and credential leak.
